Good evening. The warm bread is complimentary, on the house. Can I start you off with something to drink?
Thank you. It's our first time here -- what would you recommend for the entree?
Honestly, I'd go with the sea bass tonight -- it's the best thing we have. Or you might wanna try the tasting menu, if you'd rather let the chef choose.
My guest is gluten-free. Should I mention his dietary restriction to the kitchen now, before we order?
Yes, always now -- thank you. The sea bass is already gluten-free, so that's an easy one. What can I get for you?
He'll have the sea bass, then. For me, the steak, medium-rare, with a side dish of grilled vegetables.
Excellent choices. And why don't I send over the sommelier? He can suggest a light red that works with both plates.
Perfect. So: two sea bass -- no, one sea bass, one steak medium-rare -- and the sommelier. And could you put the whole tab on my card? It's on me.
Of course. I'll take care of everything -- and just a reminder, the tip isn't included, so you can add it at the end. Enjoy your evening.

Could we have the tab, please? It's on me -- and I'll add the tip.

Good evening, Ms. Ito, this is Diane calling from Bistro Sixty in Midtown, confirming your reservation for two at eight o'clock this Thursday. We are looking forward to having you. I have a few notes on your table, and I wanted to go over them before you arrive. First, I see that one of your guests is gluten-free. Thank you for telling us in advance. That was the right thing to do, because our chef can prepare almost the whole menu without gluten, but he needs a little time to plan it. So when you sit down, you don't have to explain it all again. Just remind your server, and the kitchen will already know. Second, a small suggestion. If it is a special dinner, you might want to try the tasting menu. It is five courses, and the chef chooses each one, so you don't have to read the whole menu or decide anything. I'd recommend it for a business dinner, because it lets you talk instead of order. And last, one nice thing. The first plate of the evening, the warm bread and the olive oil, is always complimentary at your table. See you Thursday, Ms. Ito. And if anything changes, please call us back.

Welcome back to The Confident Diner. Today, the one thing that separates a nervous tourist from someone who looks like they eat out in America every week. And it is not the food words. It is a single question. When the server comes over and the menu is a wall of words you half understand, most people freeze and point at something. Don't. Ask the question that a local always asks: what do you recommend? Four words, and suddenly the server is on your side, doing the hard part for you. And listen to how they answer, because it is never a full sentence. It is I'd go with the salmon, or you might wanna try the special, or honestly, the short rib is amazing tonight. Those little openers, I'd go with, you might wanna, why don't you, that is the whole grammar of a recommendation, and once you hear them, you hear them everywhere. Now, two things nobody tells you. One, in America the tip is not on the bill. You look at the tab, and you add eighteen to twenty percent yourself, on top. Nobody reminds you, and if you forget, it is not a small thing here. Two, when you host, you say three words at the end and you say them early, before anyone reaches for a wallet. It's on me. Say it with a smile, and the dinner is yours.
